Elusive Target: This claim is a one-off special score against a Tier 4 target, which yields +5 bonus Rep over and above the usual rewards. Having a connection to this claim means

Hit Squad Special Abilities

TAKE THE SHOT

When any PC takes the decisive action to eliminate a key target, they get +1d .

The key words here are decisive action and key target . Both must apply. Examples might include taking a sniper shot at the mark, dropping poison into the target’s drink, cutting the brakes on their autopod, tricking them into walking out on to a sabotaged balcony, dropping a chandelier, tinkering with the sauna controls to set the temperature to “lethal,” etc.

UNDER THE RADAR

When you kill someone during a score, you can take 2 Stress so it doesn’t count toward Heat afterwards. How does your crew avoid the Death Radar?

When your crew kills someone, it doesn’t trigger the Death Radar at Section 6 Central, and no klaxon sounds. Is this because of some gadget or invention, or a paranormal ritual, or a contact on the inside at Section 6?

CHAMELEONS

Take + 1d to the engagement roll for stealth or deception approaches. When you subdue someone and don their outfit, the disguise has increased effect .

The second part of this ability only applies to disguises acquired during the score—you must subdue a target like a guard or servant, and steal their uniform. You gain increased effect when you do.

COLD STORAGE

Every crew member can always find a convenient place to hide a body near to hand. Take 1 Stress to hide the body in the resonance field instead (it will rematerialize after a few hours).

Convenient hiding places might include a dumpster, locker, closet, laundry hamper, sewer grate, folded up in a folding bed, the trunk of an autopod, under a tarp, in a wine barrel, down an elevator shaft, in a portable toilet, etc.

How do you hide bodies in the resonance field? Is it a strange invention that every crew member carries, or some paranormal ritual?

BENEFACTOR

When you take this ability, the crew immediately receives 4 stacks . Whenever you advance your Tier , it costs half the stacks it normally would.

Who’s backing you? Why do they help you?

LOOPERS

Your crew is caught in an anomaly, and regularly experiences time loops where events are replayed. Usually you don’t retain memory between loops, but you can jump loops with some effort. Take 4 Stress after any action roll to roll it again, and take the best result between the two rolls . The second roll uses the exact same number of dice (pushes, assists, etc.). You can choose to do this after the GM has announced the consequences, but not after any resistance rolls.

Imagine the score playing out in splitscreen, like sliding doors. Which version of events will we follow as one becomes the main narrative?

Activating this ability costs a lot of Stress, but there are times when you will scrape together every bonus die and assist you can and still manage to roll poorly. Being able to roll the whole dice pool again to see if you can do better can be a lifesaver.

What’s the origin of this ability? Was it the unintentional side effect of something the crew was exposed to, or the result of an ingenious invention or paranormal ritual? It may even be the result of something that happens in the future, which the crew is not aware of yet.

PROFESSIONALS

When you keep an operation quiet or make it look like an accident, you get half the Rep value of the target (round up) instead of zero. When you end downtime with zero Heat, take +1 Rep .

There are many clients who value quiet operations. This ability rewards you for keeping a low profile.

Hit Squad Questions

After character questions, your group can run through crew questions together. Each PC picks one from the following list that applies to you (or make up your own) and gives a few details about how you came to that role. You don’t have to go too in-depth—this is just to give a general sense of the shape of the crew as you begin.

  • Which of you first suggested forming a crew?

  • Which of you has a grand vision for the crew’s future?

  • Which of you enjoys this a little too much?

  • Which of you still feels uneasy about it?

  • Which of you has the most to lose if we get caught?

  • Which of you knows the streets best?

Hit Squad Opportunities

During the planning meeting (page 180), each player (as well as the GM) will present an opportunity for a possible score that the crew might take. If anyone feels stuck, look at the following list and consider an Accident, Disappearance, Murder, or Ransom approach for any of them.

d6Opportunity
1A bored and petty aristocrat wants to teach their rival a lesson.
2A bankrupt aristocrat offers the last of their wealth in return for revenge on a powerful lender, who repossessed their autopod and foreclosed on their mansion.
3An arms dealer is going to defect to the USC. Before they flee Doskvol, they’re attending one more high society event. Deal with the situation.
4Target a cult leader during their large annual gathering.
5A high-stakes gambler scored big against a powerful faction, and they want payback.
6A foreign spy will be conducting a handoff with a mole at a wax museum.

Militants

Crew Type THREE of SIX MILITANTS

ACTIVISTS PUSHING FOR REVOLUTIONARY CHANGE

When you play Militants, you earn xp when you execute a successful assassination, demonstration, sabotage, or battle operation. This is a time of radical political and social upheaval—as Militants, you’re on the frontlines of the struggle.

Starting Upgrades

  • Training: Resolve

  • Cohort: Gang (Muscle)

The Cause

Instead of hunting grounds, choose two Enemies of the Cause . You get +1d to gather information rolls and a free downtime activity for preferred operations against Enemies of the Cause.

It may help to start by defining your cause, at least loosely. Is your cause civil rights, anti-imperialism, anti-war, anti-hull, freedom from Bluecoats brutality, economic justice, workers’ rights, or something else?

Enemies of the Cause might be:

  • Strangford-Michter Financial. A powerful investment firm with a habit of backing illegal ventures off the books. Has stakes in a large number of criminal groups and shady scientific projects.

  • Imperial Military. Garrisons are posted at Gaddoc Monorail Station, aboard the naval destroyer Nightcastle , and at the Premier’s stronghold (about 1,000 troops in total). The military also runs a training camp east of Doskvol for fresh conscripts, along with a prison for draft dodgers and deserters.

  • Section 6. A powerful and shadowy government agency responsible for enforcing Reality Constancy Law (investigating anomalies and unlicensed paranormal experimentation).

  • The Palace. The Imperial Intelligence Agency, or I.I.A.

  • Ministry of Stability. The governmental body that oversees border security, transportation, and the disbursement of food and other vital resources. Their pursuit of national stability also involves a program of surveillance and control.

  • The Church. They honor the life of the body and deem the transcendent, spiritual, or otherworldly to be heretical. Ruthless enforcers of conformity.

  • Bluecoats. The City Watch of Duskwall, tasked with upholding the law. Decades of PR have rebranded them as your friendly local constables, but they are still just as corrupt, violent, and cruel as ever.

  • Lusk Reform Clinic. Pioneers of the Lusk Reform Technique; they turn the most violent criminal into one of the Reformed (vacant-eyed hollows) and also produce a brand-new hull to serve the city.

  • The Foundation. A powerful consortium of property developers, architects, and builders.

  • Xantara Pharm. A profiteering pharmaceutical company.

Preferred Operations

  • Assassination. An obvious killing of a key NPC from an Enemy of the Cause faction.

  • Demonstration. A public act of defiance with symbolic meaning. Protests, occupying buildings, art projects, defacing a meaningful target.

  • Sabotage. Hurt an Enemy of the Cause by destroying some key piece of infrastructure.

  • Battle. Confront an Enemy of the Cause with direct armed resistance. Riots, clashing with law enforcement, fighting to control a key neighborhood or apartment, directly assaulting an HQ.

Contacts

  • Davran , a foreign diplomat. Perhaps a potential patron for your crew?

  • Hickling , a union organizer. Perhaps a fellow traveler, with their ear to the ground?

  • Eaves , a Palace handler. Perhaps they’re willing to put their substantial intelligence resources behind you, if your revolutionary targets align with their own shady goals?

  • Kannick , a Blackthorn Park handler. Perhaps an espionage player who sees you as an important pawn on the board?

  • Sovata , a student leader. Perhaps someone with sympathetic politics who’s able to swing grassroots support behind you?

  • Kastor , a weapons dealer. Perhaps a source for armaments and explosives?

Militant Upgrades

  • Militant Rigging: You get 2 free load worth of weapon or armor items. For example, you could be wearing armor for only 1 load, instead of 3.

  • Elite Runners: All your cohorts with the Runner type get +1d to Quality rolls for Runner-related actions.

  • Elite Muscle: All your cohorts with the Muscle type get +1d to Quality rolls for Muscle-related actions.

  • Complicated: Each PC gets +1 key/deadlock box, meaning they can each take another key (which can become another deadlock). This costs three upgrades to unlock, not just one. This may bring a PC with four deadlocks back into play if you wish.

Militant Claims

This crew has five special claims on the claim map, including two one-off opportunities . The “claim” is itself a lucrative score (with bonus rewards). If they have the connection on the claim map, the crew can attempt the one-off score at any time of their choosing. It doesn’t expire.

Catalyst Action: This claim is a oneoff score against a Tier 4 target, and if successful, it creates a new Tier 2 faction that starts with +2 status with the crew. Having access to this claim means you have everything primed, ready to light the fuse. What kind of faction will it create—a small cell that branches off your own crew, or a broad popular movement, or something in between? Which faction has to be hit to create the catalyst?

Critical Strike: This claim is a one-off opportunity against a Tier 5 target , and if successful, that target faction drops two Tiers immediately . This is a very dangerous score with a highimpact result. It’s probably the end of a season, or even the campaign. What’s the obstacle to unlocking this opportunity—learning some secret weakness of the target faction, or setting up a trap, or making sure there will be appropriate publicity?

Cover Identities: You get +1d to the engagement roll for deception and social approaches. False identities help confuse the opposition.

Covert Funding: You get +2 stacks payout scores that involve sabotage or assassination . Who’s secretly funding your fight against the Enemies of the Cause? Why?

Dispensary: You get +1d to healing treatment rolls. Easy access to all the best drugs and medicines.

Donations: You get +2 stacks payout for scores that involve battle or demonstration . Who’s donating money toward your fight against the Enemies of the Cause?

Expropriation: Unlocking this claim means that the Critical Strike one-off opportunity now includes +10 stacks in its payout . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual. What obstacle stands in the way of these resources?

Grassroot Support: Unlocking this claim means that if you complete the Catalyst Action one-off opportunity , the resultant new faction starts with +3 status with your crew. The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Hostel: Your Muscle cohorts get +1 scale . Suitable accommodation means more gang members.

Informants: You get +1d to gather information for a score. Your eyes and ears on the streets are always on the lookout for new targets.

“Interview Room”: +1d to Command and Sway on-site.

Key Conspirator: Unlocking this claim means you get +2d to the engagement roll for the Critical Strike one-off opportunity . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Meeting Hall: +1d to Consort and Sway on-site.

Popular Support : You get +1d to the engagement roll for stealth approaches. Everyday people will help cover for you.

Secret Caches: You get +1d to Acquire Asset rolls . You have secret stockpiles of various resources, which can be useful on their own, or used for barter when you need it.

Social Clubs: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

Militant Special Abilities

SYMPATHIZERS

The common citizenry of your home ground, and any turf you hold, are sympathetic to you and your cause. You can consider them allies (status +3). When asked, they will: give you shelter or a place to hide, redirect Bluecoats or other enemies, or give you other aid if they can.

MUTUAL AID

You can lend support to one allied faction, and their Tier counts as one higher as long as you do. Your crew gets one free downtime action to work on one of that faction’s long-term project clocks, if you wish. You can switch support once per downtime.

GUERRILLAS

When the target of a score is two or more Tier higher than you, take no penalty to the engagement roll, and gain +2 Rep after the score (whether you were successful or not).

Usually if the target of a score is higher Tier, the engagement roll suffers a -1 penalty. With this ability, there is no penalty (if the target is two or more Tier higher).

ON THE SIDE OF THE OPPRESSED

You loudly and openly oppose the Bluecoats and their authoritarianism. You may count each Wanted Level as if it were turf.

The maximum Wanted Level is 4. Regardless of how much turf you hold (from this ability or otherwise), the minimum Rep cost to advance your Tier is always 6.

ABSOLUTE COMMITMENT

You have all sworn solemn vows of service to the cause. You get +1d to resistance rolls.

This ability only applies to PCs in the crew. It doesn’t confer any special resistance to your cohorts.

If another crew type takes this ability as a versatile advance, they should decide what solemn vows they have sworn, and to what cause.

BENEFACTOR

When you take this ability, the crew immediately receives 4 stacks . Whenever you advance your Tier , it costs half the stacks it normally would.

Who’s backing you? Why do they help you?

REVOLUTIONARIES

When you’re at war (-3 faction status), your crew does not suffer -1 hold and PCs still get two downtime activities, instead of just one.

If another crew type takes this ability as a versatile advance, they should decide what their revolutionary cause is.

Militant Questions

After character questions, your group can run through crew questions together. Each PC picks one from the following list that applies to you (or make up your own) and gives a few details about how you came to that role. You don’t have to go too in-depth—this is just to give a general sense of the shape of the crew as you begin.

  • Which of you first suggested forming a crew?

  • Which of you is the most devout believer in the cause?

  • Which of you is the most cynical?

  • Which of you has the most personal grudge against an Enemy of the Cause?

  • Which of you knows the streets best?

  • Which of you has the most to lose if we go to Ironhook?

Militant Opportunities

During the planning meeting (page 180), each player (as well as the GM) will present an opportunity for a possible score that the crew might take. If anyone feels stuck, look at the following list and consider an Assassination, Demonstration, Sabotage, or Battle approach for any of them.

How do Militants get paid? The payout might be seized directly from the target during the score. Local supporters (victims, families, grassroots groups) might pool their savings to support the crew. A rival faction of the target may pay the militant crew, using them as a catspaw (particularly common for espionage factions, but could also be one corporation paying the crew to target another corporation, etc.). Sympathetic insiders (officials and bureaucrats) might funnel money to the crew, off the books, to do what they cannot.

d6Opportunity
1Undermine a political rally or parade staged by an Enemy of the Cause.
2Target a power transfer station, rail line, communications tower, or factory that supports an Enemy of the Cause.
3Remove or publicly shame a prominent politician, officer, financier, or other collaborator of an Enemy of the Cause.
4Tear down or deface a monument, statue, or other symbol of an Enemy of the Cause.
5Aid a strike, riot, rebellion, or walkout by providing leadership, arms, or a distraction.
6Expose a secret operation, program, or project that an Enemy of the Cause wants kept quiet.
d6Opportunity
1Seize or destroy a convoy, weapons cache, payroll, or equipment meant for an Enemy of the Cause.
2Reclaim a neighborhood. Free a street or apartment block currently patrolled or occupied by an Enemy of the Cause.
3Target propaganda infrastructure. An advertising agency, plasmovision studio, radio broadcaster, or other mouthpiece of an Enemy of the Cause.
4Free a political prisoner.
5Retaliation. Make an example of those responsible for a recent crackdown, atrocity, or other example of authoritarian brutality.
6Defend a neighborhood, spokesperson, or community group that is being threatened.

Crew Type FOUR of SIX

Racers

THRILL-SEEKERS, SMUGGLERS, DAREDEVILS, AND FAMILY RACERS

When you play Racers, you earn xp when you execute a successful smuggling, hijacking, racing, or hot pursuit operation. Are you racers first and criminals second, or the other way around? Do you have a code?

Starting Upgrades

  • Vehicles: Every PC starts with their own autopod, autocycle, or boat. A second upgrade improves the vehicles with armor and carrying capacity.

  • Lair: Workshop

Local Circuit

Instead of hunting grounds, your crew has a local circuit . Choose a preferred operation type. When you gain turf, you expand your circuit and unlock new preferred operations.

  • Smuggling. The covert transportation of illicit goods (or people) into or out of restricted areas.

  • Hijacking. Intercept and seize a vehicle or convoy and deliver it safely to a target location.

  • Racing. Either illegal street racing, or within a sanctioned autodrome. Cash prizes are involved, but there may be other shady goals for particular clients, such as fixing the race or taking out a particular target.

  • Smash-and-Grab. Hit fast and hard, grab the target, and follow it with a high-speed getaway.

Contacts

  • Karson , a bookmaker. Perhaps a good source for the latest news about illegal street races?

  • Rinse , a drug dealer. Perhaps a potential client for hijacking operations?

  • Ivory , an arms dealer. Perhaps a potential client for smuggling operations, or a source for acquiring weapons and explosives?

  • Baine , a Palace spy. Perhaps a source for high-stakes operations, such as hot pursuit?

  • Buddy , a mechanic. Perhaps a reliable place to get your autopods fixed up?

  • Vreen , owns the autodrome. Perhaps a good source for tip-offs about upcoming races?

Racer Upgrades

  • Racer Rigging: Two of your carried items are perfectly concealed. You could carry 1 load of contraband and a handcannon, perfectly concealed, even against a pat down.

  • Airship: Add mobility to your base. You can move it to a new location as a downtime activity—this thwarts any potential raid of your base that would have otherwise occurred.

  • Elite Runners: All your cohorts with the Runners type get +1d to Quality rolls for Runner-related actions.

  • Complicated: Each PC gets +1 key/deadlock box, meaning they can each take another key (which can become another deadlock). This costs three upgrades to unlock, not just one. This may bring a PC with four deadlocks back into play if you wish.

  • Heavy Vehicles: A semi-truck, bulldozer, tractor, barge, bus, drilling rig, limousine, or armored car. A second upgrade adds another heavy vehicle.

Racer Claims

This crew has five special claims on the claim map, including two one-off opportunities . The “claim” is itself a lucrative score (with bonus rewards). If they have the connection on the claim map, the crew can attempt the one-off score at any time of their choosing. It doesn’t expire.

Bets Placed: Unlocking this claim means that the Big Street Race now includes +5 stacks in its payout . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual. Who do you have to convince to make the bet for you? How do they ask you to prove yourselves?

Big Street Race: This claim is a one-off special score against Tier 3 opponents , with a 15 stack prize . What’s required to get an invite to the race—gaining the notice of a particular faction, or achieving certain victories?

Chop Shop: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

Convoy: Your cohorts have their own vehicles. Each cohort has a common vehicle, with Quality equal to your Tier.

Cover Operation: You take -2 Heat per score. What’s your cover? Who did you seize it from?

Deathlands Race: This claim is a

one-off special score against Tier 4 opponents , which yields 10 Rep and 15 stacks as a prize. This is a very dangerous opportunity with a high payout. It’s probably the end of a season, or even the campaign.

Lounge Bar: +1d to Consort and Sway on-site.

Loyal Fence: You get +2 stacks payout for smuggling or hijacking scores.

Secret Hit: Unlocking this claim means that the Deathlands Race now includes +10 stacks in its payout (if you take out a particular target during the race). The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Secret Routes: You get +1d to the engagement roll for speed approaches.

Warehouse: You get +1d to Acquire Asset rolls . You have a stockpile of various resources, which can be useful on their own, or used for barter when you need it.

Weird Old Gate: Safe passage out of the Bubble. Usually travel in and out of the Bubble is strictly controlled by the Ministry of Stability. This gate provides a safe and secret path, just for you .

Weird Old Maps: Unlocking this claim means you get +2d to the engagement roll for the Deathlands Race . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Gambling Den: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

Informants: You get +1d to gather information for a score. Your eyes and ears on the streets are always on the lookout for new targets.

Racer Special Abilities

The crew starts with Stunt Riders, and can choose one more.

STUNT RIDERS

The first time one of the crew’s signature stunts is performed during a score, take +2d and increased effect . Choose one to start with. The crew masters an additional signature stunt each time you take this ability. You can do any of these stunts even if they’re not a signature, but don’t get bonus dice etc.

Smuggler’s Turn: A controlled skid and turn before speeding off in the opposite direction.

Reverse 180: Spinning 180 degrees and continuing in the same direction, but facing backward.

Shell Game: Launching decoy vehicles (may need to acquire an asset) to confuse and surprise.

Outside Ride: Riding the roof, side, or hood of a moving vehicle, and leaping from vehicle to vehicle.

Big Air: Launching a vehicle into the air across a surprising distance with any improvised ramp.

Ramming Speed: Putting your foot down and meeting obstacles head-on.

CUSTOM RIDE

Create one of your vehicles as a cohort (use the vehicle edges and flaws in the following list). Its Quality is equal to your Tier +1. The vehicle can use teamwork actions (using Quality for rolls). A vehicle can’t lead a group action, but may participate. A vehicle can help during downtime like other cohorts, but cannot go on secondary scores.

Vehicle Edges

Nimble: The vehicle handles easily. Consider this an assist (+1d) for tricky maneuvers.

Simple: The vehicle is easy to repair. Remove all its harm during downtime. Sturdy: The vehicle keeps operating even when broken.

Vehicle Flaws

Guzzler: The vehicle costs 1 stack per downtime to keep in operation.

Distinct: The vehicle has memorable features. Take +1 Heat when you use it on a score.

Finicky: When operated without the one person who understands it, the vehicle has -1 Quality.

The Blackline Syndicate, a Tier 1 crew of racers, have a vehicle cohort (a sweet van called the Black Echo). Aniya is driving the Black Echo in a race. She could rely purely on the van’s Quality, making a 2d fortune roll (Tier +1) to see if it can take the lead by itself. Instead she decides to lead a group action, with her driving skill and the van itself both contributing. She rolls Finesse and pushes herself for a total of 3d. The van roll its Quality (2d). Aniya’s highest result is a 1, while the van’s highest result is a 5. Aniya takes 1 Stress for the 1-3 result, but the 4/5 result stands. The Black Echo takes the lead, but there will be a consequence that could apply to either Aniya, her van, or both.

ESCAPE VELOCITY

One of the crew’s vehicles is fitted with a device that activates at a specific speed. This requires filling an 8-segment acceleration clock during the score. When the clock is filled and the vehicle hits the target speed, choose an effect: break the laws of gravity—create a tear in reality—travel into the resonance field—travel backward or forward through time.

If the vehicle comes to a stop, reset the acceleration clock to zero.

THE FAMILY TAKES A CUT

Before payout for a smuggling or hijacking score, the crew can skim the goods for +4 stacks . The GM will tick a clock.

The GM can tick any clock of their choice—a good default is “somebody notices you’re skimming the goods.” The idea is that any repercussions are not immediate, and won’t kick in until the clock is filled. Also, you can work against that clock during downtime with your own long-term projects, etc.

BRING THEM IN

As long as you’re not currently fighting them (e.g. you’re at war), you can turn even the most bitter of enemies into family. This requires a long-term project— 6 segments to turn them into a crew contact, 10 segments to turn them into a cohort or PC (your choice). If they become a PC, create them as normal, with bonus action dots and abilities each equal to their Tier, and any deadlocks at the GM’s discretion. Their faction’s relationship to your crew does not change.

SPEED DEMONS

When you go into conflict aboard a vehicle, you gain increased effect for vehicle damage and speed. If the vehicle has armor, you can use it for your own protection.

Racer Questions

After character questions, your group can run through crew questions together. Each PC picks one from the following list that applies to you (or make up your own) and gives a few details about how you came to that role. You don’t have to go too in-depth—this is just to give a general sense of the shape of the crew as you begin.

  • Which of you first suggested forming a crew?

  • Which of you has been racing the longest?

  • Which of you has experience smuggling?

  • Which of you drives like you have a death wish?

  • Which of you knows the streets best?

  • Which of you has the most to lose if we get caught?

Ways to Run a Race

If the players decide to be a crew of Racers, the GM will want to think about how to run races. There is no one correct way; try something in one score and see how it feels, then tweak it for next time.

Follow the Fiction

You could follow the race in the fiction, keeping track of the relative position of the various competitors either in your head, with pen and paper, with tokens, or with miniature cars.

Successful action rolls will help PCs pull ahead, while consequences might push them back and see them overtaken. You could use a simple clock (for example, an 8-clock) to represent how long the race is, ticking it with every action roll. When the clock is complete, whoever is in the lead wins.

Can one of the PCs make sure they’re in the front of the pack at the right time?

Racing Clocks

You could use racing clocks (see Blades in the Dark , page 16). Put down two opposed clocks—one for a PC winning the race, and one for someone else winning.

Successes put ticks on the “Win the Race” clock (with the number of ticks depending on effect, as usual). Consequences put ticks on the clock for “Someone Else Wins.” Can the PCs finish their “Win the Race” clock before someone else wins?

Start simple with an 8-clock for both. With their various advantages, abilities, and resisting consequences, the PCs should win pretty easily.

Next time, you could try an 8-clock for the PCs and a 6-clock for the opposition. Keep making the PC clock bigger and the opposition clock smaller for more challenging races. Also remember that against higher-Tier opponents, the PCs will have less effect (meaning fewer clock ticks on a success).

Racer Opportunities

During the planning meeting (page 180), each player (as well as the GM) will present an opportunity for a possible score that the crew might take. If anyone feels stuck, look at the following list and consider a Smuggling, Hijacking, Racing, or Hot Pursuit approach for any of them.

d6Opportunity
1Rumor is that anyone who wins big at the autodrome gets approached about race-fixing. A client wants you to win big and tell them who approaches you.
2A joyrider stole a luxury autopod belonging to a very wealthy, powerful individual. They want it back.
3The Deep Run, a street race that goes into the depths of Coalridge Deep Excavation and back out. Strange shit happens down there, but the prize money is real.
4A defecting military officer needs urgent passage out of the city.
5A paranormal crystal (a prison for a powerful entity) is scheduled to be transported out of the city and into a containment facility in the deathlands. A weird old recluse desperately wants that crystal for himself.
6The Highwire, a deadly street race that takes place entirely across the rooftops of Doskvol.
d6Opportunity
1A corporate R&D lab is offering a cash bounty to anyone who can beat their experimental new autopod in a race.
2The Voidwater Burn, a street race along bridges, barges, and many miles of canals.
3A rival outfit is transporting contraband through the deathlands in a customized tanker. They say it’s invulnerable; prove them wrong.
4The Bowmore Royale, an illegal demolition derby where competitors deliberately try to destroy each other’s vehicles in a large arena, until only one is left standing.
5A powerful figure just found out their rakish lover has been brazenly cheating on them. The former is furious and wants revenge, so the scoundrel is trying to hightail it out of the city.
6A self-driving autopod powered by a sparkmind intelligence has gone rogue. Nobody has managed to catch it.

Crew Type FIVE of SIX

COVERT SPECIALISTS, EXPERTS IN HEISTS AND ESPIONAGE

Shadows

When you play a Shadows, you earn xp when you execute a successful surveillance, heist, subterfuge, or sabotage score. Do you steal to line your own pockets, or do you have a larger project? Do you have any allegiances, or do you work for the highest bidder?

Starting Upgrades

  • Lair: Hidden

  • Cohort: Gang (Infiltrators)

Hunting Grounds

Choose a favored operation type. This will color what kind of Shadows you are — smooth operators who pull off daring heists, or shady professionals who specialize in espionage intelligence? When you gain turf, you unlock new preferred operations.

  • Surveillance. Gather intelligence for a client through stakeouts, wiretapping, planting bugs, or following a target.

  • Heists. The theft of valuables, whether the target is art and jewelery, bank vaults, private collections, or casinos.

  • Subterfuge. Covert infiltration, compromising secure facilities, conducting clandestine meetings, and other acts of espionage.

  • Cleanup. Rogue agents, cover-ups gone wrong, failed ops; there’s good money in being the ones who tidy up loose ends, hide the evidence, and extract compromised assets.

Contacts

  • Malloy , a Ministry clerk. Perhaps a good source for tip-offs about potential scores?

  • Caldwell , an art forger. Perhaps a useful tool in your toolbox?

  • Bloom , a collector. Perhaps a potential patron for your crew?

  • Vera , a switchboard operator. Perhaps someone who knows what the powerful talk about on closed lines?

  • Catton , a hotel concierge. Perhaps a good source of news and gossip?

  • Tarnish , an information broker. Perhaps a well-connected figure in the espionage world, known for their neutrality?

Shadows Upgrades

  • Secret maps, and various keys and passcards: You have easy passage through the city’s shadow routes—underground canals, service tunnels, and basement access hatches.

  • Elite Infiltrators: All your cohorts with the Infiltrators type get +1d to Quality rolls for Infiltrator-related actions.

  • Elite Hustlers: All your cohorts with the Hustlers type get +1d to Quality rolls for Hustler-related actions.

  • Multifaceted: Each PC gets +1 key/deadlock box, meaning they can each take another key (which can become another deadlock). This costs three upgrades to unlock, not just one. This may bring a PC with four deadlocks back into play if you wish.

Shadows Claims

This crew has five special claims on the claim map, including two one-off opportunities . The “claim” is itself a lucrative score (with bonus rewards). If they have the connection on the claim map, the crew can attempt the one-off score at any time of their choosing. It doesn’t expire.

Checkmate: This claim is a one-off special score against a Tier 5 target , which yields 30 stacks as payout, and completes any clock of the crew’s choice. Consider the fiction when choosing the clock—the idea is that by completing this score, the crew finishes something important like stopping a ticking timebomb, exposing a conspiracy, compromising a high-Tier faction, etc. This is a very dangerous opportunity with a big reward. It’s probably the end of a season, or even the campaign.

Cover Identities: You get +1d to the engagement roll for deception and social approaches. False identities help confuse the opposition.

Cover Story: Unlocking this claim means you get +2d to the engagement roll for the Checkmate score . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Covert Fixer: You get +2 stacks payout for surveillance or sabotage scores.

Critical Move: This claim is a one-off special score against a Tier 4 target , which yields a 15 stack payout and puts +4 ticks on any clock of the crew’s choice. Consider the fiction when choosing the clock—the idea is that by completing this score, the crew works toward something important like stopping a ticking timebomb, exposing a conspiracy, compromising a high-Tier faction, etc.

Dispensary: You get +1d to healing treatment rolls . Easy access to all the best drugs and medicines.

High Stakes Game: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

Inside Mark: Unlocking this claim means that the target for the Checkmate counts as -1 Tier for that score. The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Informants: You get +1d to gather information for a score. Your eyes and ears on the streets are always on the lookout for new targets.

Interested Party: Unlocking this claim means that the Checkmate score now includes +10 stacks in its payout . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Lounge Bar: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

Luxury Venue: +1d to Command and Sway on-site.

Paid-Off Bluecoats: You take -2 Heat per score.

Secret Pathways: You get +1d to the engagement roll for stealth plans. You might have access to long-forgotten underground canals, rooftop walkways, or some other route of your choosing.

Slush Fund: You get +1d to Acquire Asset rolls . You have access to a secret, off-the-books account. Who owns it, and why have they given you access?

Specialist Fence: You get +2 stacks payout for heists or subterfuge scores.

Shadows Special Abilities

SYNCHRONIZED WATCHES

You may use assists or setup actions with any crew member, regardless of the distance separating you. When you perform a group action , you may count multiple 6s from different rolls as a critical success.

The first part of this ability only works with assists or setup actions, not group actions. The second part of this ability works with group actions.

THIEVES WITH A CODE

When you complete a score with no deaths having occurred, gain +1 faction status with the local citizenry.

SLEEPY TIME

Everyone on the crew adds tranquilizer rounds (0 load, compatible with any firearm) to their playbook items.

Each character gets one use of these tranq rounds—so if you use them in your silenced shooter, they won’t be available for your handcannon. The GM may use the crew Tier as a fortune roll to see how long the effect lasts, sometimes.

DON’T FORGET THE DUFFEL BAG

Once per score, the crew can flashback to an Acquire Asset roll with +2d . Both the flashback and the downtime activity are free.

You may still wish to spend stacks to increase the result level of the Acquire Asset roll.

BENEFACTOR

When you take this ability, the crew immediately receives 4 stacks . Whenever you advance your Tier , it costs half the stacks it normally would.

Who’s backing you? Why do they help you?

ECHO SAFEHOUSE

The crew has access to a safehouse in the resonance field. It can be accessed once per score, from anywhere. The door always returns to the same place it was entered from. Use it as a hiding place, to reequip, repair, or just take a breather. Any PCs who enter gain +1 load . One PC may clear all level 1 harm via emergency medical supplies there. Note that time still passes in the outside world.

BLACK BAG OPERATIONS

When you execute a clandestine infiltration, you get +1d to the engagement roll . You may attempt to install surveillance equipment during a score on any faction’s turf. So long as the wire remains in place, take +1d on any future gather information rolls related to that faction. The GM may make a fortune roll using the target faction’s Tier to see if they notice the bug and remove it during any personal business phase.

Shadows Questions

After character questions, your group can run through crew questions together. Each PC picks one from the following list that applies to you (or make up your own) and gives a few details about how you came to that role. You don’t have to go too in-depth—this is just to give a general sense of the shape of the crew as you begin.

  • Which of you first suggested forming a crew?

  • Which of you has the most experience with heists?

  • Which of you was recruited for your particular skill set?

  • Which of you handles the homework—making the maps, charts, and diagrams before a heist?

  • Which of you looks after fencing the goods, and maintains the books?

  • Which of you knows the streets best?

Shadows Opportunities

During the planning meeting (page 180), each player (as well as the GM) will present an opportunity for a possible score that the crew might take. If anyone feels stuck, look at the following list and consider a Surveillance, Heist, Subterfuge, or Sabotage approach for any of them.

d6Opportunity
1A private art dealer has acquired something they shouldn’t have.
2A new nightclub is rumored to be a front for an intelligence agency.
3A courier has gotten sloppy and uses the same canal route to carry sensitive documents from Charterhall, every Home Day.
4The consulate gala will feature high-profile guests, loose lips, and lax security. 5 A defecting scientist wants extraction—but they’re under constant watch. 6 An informant is ready to talk, but only to someone off the books.
d6Opportunity
1The target of a political assassination has resurfaced as a resonance echo. Finish the job, permanently.
2A casino is hosting the biggest high-roller tournament of the year. The prize pool is eye-watering.
3There’s a train leaving Gaddoc Station with a suspected intelligence handoff to occur during the journey.
4You know the time and location of an infamous, clandestine underworld auction, held once every three years.
5An assassination attempt failed—the target wants to know who ordered it, and they want payback.
6An airship belonging to a millionaire recluse will be docked at Penderyn Air Terminal, for just one night.

A NEW AGE CULT DEDICATED TO NEW AGE CULT DEDICATED TO DEDICATED TO AN UNSETTLING VISION OF THE FUTUREVISION OF THE FUTUREOF THE FUTUREFUTURE

Crew Type A NEW AGE CULT DEDICATED TO NEW AGE CULT DEDICATED TO DEDICATED TO SIX of SIX AN UNSETTLING VISION OF THE FUTUREVISION OF THE FUTUREOF THE FUTUREFUTURE UTOPIANS

Utopians

When you play Utopians, you earn xp when you advance the realization of your Utopian Vision. While you might start off with good (utopian) intentions, make no mistake that this crew is on a dark path. This is a cult, and there are forgotten gods just waiting for believers like you. Are your characters fully aware of the dark path they walk, or are they earnest optimists who will learn the truth of the world the hard way?

Starting Upgrades

  • Training: Resolve

  • Cohort: Gang (Eggheads)

Utopian Vision

Core to the Utopian crew is their Utopian Vision. During crew creation, choose two truths of the Utopian Vision: Free—Egalitarian—Abundant—Beautiful— Serene—Radiant—Natural.

To begin with, the Utopian Vision is somewhat vague. You do not know if it is the future or the past, or another plane of existence entirely. Importantly, you believe that it is a real place or state of being that you can actually attain .

It may help to decide how you collectively learned of this Utopian Vision. Was it a literal vision experienced by one or more of the PCs? Was it something you read about it in an ancient forbidden book? Was it something hinted at in the results of a fringe scientific experiment, or seen on the printouts from a huge and mysterious computer?

Utopians, Ward Bosses, and the Underworld

Utopians have a ward boss as usual (see page 89), and also earn Heat and Wanted Levels. It’s important to remember that the Imperium is under Reality Constancy Law, strictly enforced by Section 6. Paranormal experiments and intentionally interacting with anomalies and/or Utopian Visions are as highly illegal as murder or dealing contraband. When you undertake this sort of criminal activity in a given district, the local ward boss will expect a cut of your profits, just like they would any other crew.

Anomalies

Instead of hunting grounds, paranormal anomalies are key to manifesting your utopia. An anomaly is a localized tear in the fabric of reality, where paranormal power is amplified and anything is possible. There are three types of scores involving anomalies:

  • Discovery. Find new anomalies before they’re claimed by another faction, or secured and sealed by Section 6.

  • Stabilization. Anomalies are highly unstable by nature, but they can be harnessed through occult ritual or scientific apparatus.

  • Experimentation. The possible use cases for the immense power of an anomaly are infinite. Trial and error is the only way to know more.

Unlike other crew types who choose just one favored operation to begin with, Utopians get +1d to gather information rolls and a free downtime activity for operations that involve any of these operations.

What is the purpose of anomalies? That depends on the nature of the crew and your Utopian Vision. Anomalies break the rules of reality in a fundamental way (see page 332). They can be stabilized, harnessed, and controlled (through the three types of scores or through a long-term project), for possible effects that might include:

  • Harvesting alluvium. Rare, unstable, and very expensive on the black market, alluvium breaks the laws of reality by its very presence, and most advanced paranormal experiments require some measure of alluvium as a catalyst. Anomalies can be used as a source of alluvium.

  • Communicating with, and possibly binding, resonance entities.

  • Changing the past, or the future.

  • Changing the world. Since anomalies variously effect everything from gravity, to plant growth, to temperature, to time itself (page 332), the possibilities are endless. You might change the world to match your Utopian Vision (freeing prisoners from captivity, painting the sky in beautiful and terrifying colors, causing abundant plant growth, etc.). For example, some anomalies create an anti-gravity effect in their vicinity; this anomalous effect could be harnessed to create flying autopods.

Circles

Unlike other crew types, Utopian claims and special abilities are gated behind certain thresholds. These are arranged in four Circles of descending order. The crew starts in the First Circle, and can attempt to grab any of the First Circle claims in any order, and can take any of the First Circle special abilities.

Note that the Circles represent increasing darkness , not necessarily increasing power level. Descending to lower Circles is a story beat about the crew doing ever-darker deeds and learning ever-darker truths. It’s not as simple as “advancement”; it’s about corruption.

First Circle. The crew learns the first two truths of their Utopian Vision (perhaps: Free—Egalitarian—Abundant—Beautiful—Serene—Radiant— Natural ).

Second Circle. Requires unlocking the Threshold of Gold : when the crew has earned a total of 20 stacks (track this from the start). The crew gets a slightly clearer Utopian Vision, and learns an unsettling new truth (perhaps: Shifting— Sweltering—Lustful—Unsettling—Future—Past—Alien ).

Third Circle. Requires unlocking the Second Circle and the Threshold of Blood : when the crew has ended at least 10 lives (track this from the start of the campaign, not from when they achieve the Second Circle). The crew gets a slightly clearer Utopian Vision, and learns a dark new truth (perhaps: Psychedelic—Inhuman—Violent—Future—Past—Tempestuous ).

Final Circle. Requires unlocking the Third Circle and the Threshold of Power : the crew must be at least Tier 3 , and have created a major anomaly . The Utopian Vision becomes clear, and the crew learns a final, terrible truth (perhaps: Fiery—Frozen—Terrifying—Cruel—Monstrous—Capricious— Inhuman ).

Circles must be completed in descending order, even if their prerequisites are met early.

Only First Circle abilities may be taken as versatile advances by other crew types. If another crew type discovers their own Utopian Vision, they may switch to the Utopian crew sheet and proceed from there.

Contacts

  • Evangeline , a spiritual sage. Perhaps a charismatic cult leader of their own who can offer wise advice?

  • Friswell , an aristocrat. Perhaps bored, wealthy, and looking for deeper meaning—their money and connections could be very useful...

  • Netherley , a psychedelic artist. Perhaps their art can offer deeper insights into your Utopian Vision?

  • Tarn , a paranormal experimentalist. Perhaps a fringe scientist with their own utopian aspirations?

  • Lockmore Wend , a celebrity. Perhaps an actor or musician seeking spiritual fulfillment—could their fame be used as a beacon to spread the light of the Utopian Vision?

  • Om , a deathlands dropout. They rejected mainstream society to live in the deathlands. The revelations they’ve experienced there might be critical to your project.

Utopian Upgrades

  • Utopian Rigging: You get 2 free load worth of documents or paranormal implements. For example, you could carry a ream of printouts from a cursed terminal, as well as an echo containment unit and safety gloves, all for zero load.

  • Elite Eggheads: All your cohorts with the Eggheads type get +1d to Quality rolls for Egghead-related actions.

  • Elite Muscle: All your cohorts with the Muscle type get +1d to Quality rolls for Muscle-related actions.

  • Awakened : Each PC gets +1 key/deadlock box, meaning they can each take another key (which can become another deadlock). This costs three upgrades to unlock, not just one. This may bring a PC with four deadlocks back into play if you wish.

FIRST CIRCLE CLAIMS

Luxury Retreat: Your Egghead cohorts get +1 scale . A luxury retreat is a great way to recruit new followers.

Turf: Unlocking turf simply reduces the Rep cost to develop by one (Utopians start with all three favored operations).

Wellness Class: Any time during personal business, roll dice equal to your Tier. You earn stacks equal to the highest result, minus your Heat .

SECOND CIRCLE CLAIMS

Donations: You get +2 stacks payout for paranormal scores. What kind of pyramid scheme have you set up?

Natural Healer: You get +1d to healing treatment rolls. What form of healing do they practice?

Temple: +1d to Command and Sway on-site.

THIRD CIRCLE CLAIMS

Abandoned Lab: You get +1d to any Attune rolls on-site; both action rolls, as well downtime activities such as long-term projects. Where is this lab? What was its original purpose?

Ancient Altar: You get +1d to the engagement roll for paranormal approaches. Who, or what, is it an altar to?

Ancient Gate: Safe passage out of the Bubble. Usually travel in and out of the Bubble is strictly controlled by the Ministry of Stability. This gate provides a safe and secret path, just for you.

Ancient Tower: You get +1d to Consort with resonance entities on-site. Where is this tower? Does it even exist in our reality? Is it from the ancient past, or the ancient future?

Reality Shift: This claim is a one-off score against a Tier 4 target , and if successful, it creates a new major anomaly . The anomaly will be under

your control, and stabilized. What is the nature of the anomaly, and where is it? How is the target faction gate-keeping this possible anomaly? What will the catalyst to its creation be?

FINAL CIRCLE CLAIMS

Ancient Tomes: Unlocking this claim means you get +2d to the engagement roll for the Reality Tear one-off opportunity . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Figurehead: You convince a patsy to become the figurehead leader of your cult. At any time, have them take the fall and go to Ironhook (or worse) to remove 3 Wanted Levels from the crew. They are in too deep to seek retribution, and you will suffer no blowback.

Interested Party: Unlocking this claim means that the Reality Tear one-off opportunity now includes +6 stacks in its payout . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual.

Notable Skeptic: Unlocking this claim means that the Reality Tear one-off opportunity now yields +4 Rep . The score to unlock this claim should also carry a standard payout as usual. Who is this skeptic? Unlocking this claim means convincing them to pay attention and take you seriously.

Reality Tear: This claim is a one-off opportunity against a Tier 5 target , and if successful, it creates a new mega anomaly . A mega anomaly is a tear in reality the size of an entire district, or even multiple districts. This is a very dangerous score with a high-impact result. It’s probably the end of a season, or even the campaign. What is the nature of the anomaly, and where will it appear? How is the target faction gatekeeping this possible anomaly? What will the catalyst to its creation be?

Utopian Claims

This crew has six special claims on the claim map, including two one-off opportunities . The “claim” is itself a special score with bonus rewards.

Utopian Special Abilities

FIRST CIRCLE ABILITIES

ENLIGHTENED

You get +1d to resistance rolls against paranormal threats and consequences. You get +1d to healing rolls when you have paranormal harm.

COMMUNE

You may use teamwork with any crew member, regardless of the distance separating you. By taking 1 Stress, your projected thoughts are heard by every crew member as telepathic communication.

How does this ability work for your crew? Is it through uncanny invention, or spiritual technique? How are new crew members given this ability?

BEST INTENTIONS

If every PC vows not to kill anyone before a score, take +1d to the engagement roll. The first time each PC takes an action that might kill someone if it’s successful (whether intentional by the PC or not), they take +1d and potency . If you all keep the vow, the crew marks 1 xp .

This ability is win-win—either you keep the vow and everyone marks xp, or you break the vow and get a decent boost to one action roll. Either way, it’s the thought that counts, isn’t it? Accidents happen.

ZEAL

Every PC gets +1d and potency when they evangelize about the Utopian Vision.

The evangelizing (whether that’s Consort, Sway, Command, or whatever) must be the point of the action—this ability doesn’t apply if you happen to be evangelizing while you do something unrelated.

SECOND CIRCLE ABILITIES

TRUE BELIEVERS

Your cohorts will undertake any service, no matter how dangerous or strange. They gain +1d to rolls against enemies of the Utopian Vision.

ALTERED STATES

Each PC gains an additional vice : Ritual Hedonism . When you indulge this vice, you don’t overindulge if you clear excess Stress. In addition, your ecstatic visions of the future mean you can perform one flashback for free.

THIRD CIRCLE ABILITIES

FOR THE GREATER GOOD

A human sacrifice removes the Stress cost for any paranormal experiment you perform, and such sacrifices performed in your base do not trigger the Death Radar or generate Heat.

For details on paranormal experiments, see page 167.

THIS IS FINE

If every PC works together (group action), you can summon a denizen of your Utopian Vision into this reality; a Class V Resonance Entity.

This non-human entity (a horror or aberration) counts as Tier 5 for the purposes of fortune rolls. Note that it does not automatically obey your commands (but you may still attempt to have it do so). It does not automatically return to the resonance field.

FINAL CIRCLE ABILITIES

REMEMBER THE FORGOTTEN

You can now communicate with the entity that created your Utopian Vision (a “god”?). Tread carefully.

PARADISE MANIFEST

When you take this advance, your base and the area around transforms into a small fragment of your utopia, manifest in this reality. Take +2d to the engagement roll for any raids. All crew members gain potency when acting in the base and its surrounds, and all downtime actions conducted in the base get +1 result level . This will attract significant attention from Section 6 and other high-Tier factions.

WE NO LONGER PLAY BY YOUR RULES

When you take this advance, immediately pick two more advances.

Utopian Questions

After character questions, your group can run through crew questions together. Each PC picks one from the following list that applies to you (or make up your own) and gives a few details about how you came to that role. You don’t have to go too in-depth—this is just to give a general sense of the shape of the crew as you begin.

  • Which of you first had a vision of the utopia?

  • Which of you suggested forming a crew?

  • Which of you is the most devout believer?

  • Which of you is the most naive?

  • Which of you is the most cynical?

  • Which of you handles recruitment?

Utopian Opportunities

During the planning meeting (page 180), each player (as well as the GM) will present an opportunity for a possible score that the crew might take. If anyone feels stuck, look at the list on the next page.

How do Utopians get paid? They might sell a paranormal byproduct, such as alluvium, to a third-party client. A client might pay them to accomplish some other task in the course of their paranormal activities, such as reaching out to a dead loved one through an anomaly. A paranormal faction might pay them to do a paranormal score in exchange for sharing the data from the “experiment.” Utopians may also perform regular crimes like heists or intelligence espionage, in order to fund their more important work of realizing their Utopian Vision.

d6Opportunity
1An apartment block is suffering under the effects of an uncontrolled anomaly in the building. They’re trying to keep it secret from Section 6, to avoid a heavy-handed crackdown.
2A wealthy businessperson will pay anything to hear from their dead lover.
3Lady Risbury is holding a gallery showing of pre-cataclysm artifacts. One of them is the real deal.
4A computer terminal in the depths of the Ministry of Discovery started spewing hundreds of yards of printout, all of it about your exact Utopian Vision. What else could it tell you?
5A rival cult is assimilating smaller rivals, and killing those who refuse. Your crew may be next.
6A local street gang is plagued by the echoes of their victims. They’re desperate for help.
d6Opportunity
1You saw a sparkmind construct in your Utopian Vision. You don’t know why, but you know you must free the huge machine, and have it join your crew.
2Sirens outside. Section 6 are in the district now , on their way to a newly discovered anomaly. Can you misdirect them, and claim it for yourselves?
3Captain Blanchet says that when he takes his submarine to a particular spot 15 fathoms deep, something weird happens. Could be an anomaly down there.
4A research group at the university has successfully created an anomaly in lab conditions. A lot of people might want those findings, and the equipment.
5Section 6 has set up a temporary field office to deal with a local paranormal outbreak. Although heavily guarded, it contains top secret intel on all the reported anomalies in the district...
6There’s a student society engaging in all sorts of hedonistic, psychedelic experimentation, and they’re ripe for recruitment to one paranormal cult or another. Get there first, and demonstrate your Utopian Vision.
System Changes

Overview

lades ’68 is a supplement for Blades in the Dark and uses almost all the same core mechanics. You can swap in any modules from Blades in the B Dark: Deep Cuts. You could easily replace the action roll with the threat roll, for example.

There are many changes to the gameplay cycle , score aftermath, entanglements, and downtime—these systems are covered in full in the Gameplay Cycle chapters (page 162). There are also a few core mechanics changed to reflect the different tone of Blades ’68 .

  • Harm (page 152). Harm is just another consideration that factors into effect, and carries no other mechanical penalties. Instead, PCs roll for harm complications (page 192) after the score to see how they are impacted.

  • Resistance (page 153). You can resist any stepped consequence, such as harm or clock ticks, multiple times (reducing the consequence once each time). Armor reduces all incoming harm, but takes 3 load.

  • Keys and deadlocks (page 154). Keys are character traits. You can get 1 xp per session if your character does something that expresses one of their keys (like someone with the “charming” key trying to be charming). Pick two or three to start with. Deadlocks replace trauma. Like trauma, you take a deadlock when you run out of Stress, and you can gain xp from them, and you have to retire when you have four deadlocks.

  • Stacks and bank (page 158). Instead of coin and stash, characters track stacks of cash, and can put stacks in the bank .

  • Advancement (page 160). Desperate actions earn xp as usual, but so does rolling a zero-dot action —any action you have no rating in (if you then gain some dice from pushing or help, you still mark xp). An action that’s both desperate and zero-dot earns 2 xp. Mark 1 xp when you hit a key , once per session. There are also two end-of-session triggers on your character sheet. In all these cases, including desperate actions or zero-dot actions, you can mark the xp on any track (an attribute like Prowess, or on your playbook xp track).

Harm

Harm is just another fictional consideration that flows into effect, and carries no other mechanical penalties during a score.

The GM should weigh up a character’s harm and consider whether they might have less effect than normal for any given action. Also consider the tone of your game—these characters are daring scoundrels engaged in hectic criminal capers, and may not always be slowed down by regular injuries.

Hadwig is trying to run away from the Bluecoats, but she has the critical harm “Gunshot Wound in Leg.” The GM decides it means limited effect.

Pendleton is trying to convince the bouncer to let the crew into the nightclub. They have two level 1 harms (“Stressed” and “Dazed”), but everybody at the table has forgotten about the harm and it doesn’t really matter anyway, so it doesn’t change their effect.

If you take a mortal injury (level 4), your character will die very quickly without immediate and miraculous intervention. Write the nature of the lethal injury on your character sheet. If they happen to survive, it will surely leave a gnarly scar. If they die, they might come back as resonance echo, and their death wound will be part of their look.

Harm Complications

The main way that harm hampers a character is through potential harm complications after the score.

Immediately after the score, as part of the aftermath, every character with harm rolls for harm complications.

See Aftermath , page 192, for harm complications.

Resistance

You can resist any stepped consequence, such as harm or clock ticks,

multiple times (reducing the consequence once each time). You can decide after each resistance roll, but make it quick.

Resistance rolls will often resist the entire consequence in one go, at the GM’s discretion; they don’t always require multiple rolls.

Armor

Armor is rare but not unknown on the streets of Doskvol ’68. Riotcoats, for example, wear an outfit that includes layers of impact-resistant fabric combined with areas of rigid plate, along with a full-face helmet.

Armor costs 3 load. Hulls have built-in armor, which takes zero load.

As long as you’re wearing armor, all physical harm is reduced by one level (think of it as an automatic resistance roll), every time.

Totaled: You can choose to total your armor, which completely resists one instance of physical harm, but the armor is trashed in the process and won’t be any benefit for the rest of the score.

If you take level 3 physical harm (after the effect of the armor and any resistance rolls are applied), the armor is totaled automatically (which also resists the harm in the process).

The GM has final say whether armor is applicable to any given form of harm (e.g. poison gas).

Like other items, armor is restored during personal business.

Stab-Proof Vests and Other Light Armor

Light armor takes less load (2 slots) and is effective against just one instance of physical harm. It eliminates that harm consequence entirely, and is then totaled. Such armor can only be attained through an Acquire Asset downtime activity, or an invention project.

See page 174 for this and other example assets.

Keys and Deadlocks

Keys and Deadlocks in Brief

See the next page for a full explanation of keys and deadlocks. If you’re playing with people already familiar with Blades in the Dark , you could simply say:

  • Keys are character traits. You can get 1 xp per session if your character does something that expresses one of their keys (like someone with the “charming” key trying to be charming). Pick two or three to start with.

  • Deadlocks replace trauma. Like trauma, you take a deadlock when you run out of Stress, and you can gain xp from them, and you have to retire when you have four deadlocks.

Leave the rest of the details (marking and turning keys) until they come up in play.

Keys

Keys are the personality traits of your character. They help you decide what kind of person your PC is before you start. You get xp for portraying your keys in play.

Keys include personality traits like protective, confident, curious, kind, reckless, enthusiastic, brooding, shy.

You can have up to four keys, but you don’t need all four to start. Most people discover who their character is through play, and it’s good to leave space to do so.

There are example keys at the bottom of each playbook sheet; pick from these, or use ones from another playbook, or make up your own.

Keys are not a moral judgment. They are not “good” or “bad,” they’re just who your character is. Change your keys at any time if you think a different one would fit better.

Hitting a Key

Once per session when you showcase one of your keys through play, tell the rest of the table, mark the key on your character sheet, and mark 1 xp on any track.

Charming

That’s a maximum of one key, once per session, for a total of 1 xp from your keys per session.

You can and should roleplay all your keys all the time, but it’s still just 1 xp per session.

When a key has been marked three times, you can continue to get xp for it, but you don’t need to keep marking it.

Tell the table when you’ve hit a key, and mark it immediately. If you forget to do this during the session, it’s fine to do it retroactively when you’re going over end-of-session xp. Tell everyone what it was, and mark the key and xp as usual.

Deadlocks

When you run out of Stress (marking the last Stress box), your character has reached a moment of crisis. They’re out of the action (for a few moments, or maybe the rest of the scene), but once they’ve gathered themself, they come back with zero Stress.

When this happens, mark the explosion box next to a key.

Charming

That key is now deadlocked; cross it out. You can no longer mark it for xp, and it no longer applies to your character. Instead, write a corresponding deadlock .

Charming
Cold

A deadlock should relate to its key in some way; perhaps the opposite trait, or perhaps an intensification. Either way, a character trait that was core to your character’s career as a scoundrel has been changed forever.

Example deadlocks:

  • The key idealistic could flip to the deadlock cynical , or it might intensify into zealous .

  • The key sophisticated could flip to the deadlock modest , or it might intensify into pretentious .

  • The key commanding could flip to the deadlock deferential , or it might intensify into controlling .

Once all four keys are deadlocked, your character can’t continue in a life of crime and must retire.

Deadlocks are not always bad traits. If you have the key of distant , it might deadlock to warm —that’s a positive change for your character. What’s important, though, is that deadlocks make it more difficult for your character to continue their life of crime. Perhaps your PC needed to be distant to navigate the underworld, and becoming a warm person makes this more difficult.

The process of keys becoming deadlocks forms an important part of your character’s story arc, so consider the eventual deadlocks when choosing keys. Think not just about who your character is to begin with, but who they might become.

You may be able to convert deadlocks back into keys (either the original key, or a new one). Negotiate a long-term project with the GM to do so, depending on the nature of the deadlock and how your character goes about it.

If a deadlock turns back into a key, clear all the marks next to it.

Turning a Key

If you’ve hit a key three times (and marked the three boxes), you can choose to turn the key at any time.

Cautious

When you turn a key, you take decisive action that reflects a major change of heart. Deadlock the key immediately and clear all your Stress, and the action you take when you turn the key is automatically a critical success (with no need to roll). You must decide before you roll any dice, not after.

Cautious
Reckless

Because keys have to be deadlocked anyway when you run out of Stress, it may be worth looking for a dramatic opportunity to turn a key instead of waiting for a resistance roll to go bad.

Pendleton, a Swinger, was trying to a charm Veretta Keel but rolled a 1-3. They managed to resist being thrown out the window, but they did get slammed against the wall and they’re currently sitting on the floor, stunned. Keri marks Pendleton’s last Stress box, and has to choose a key to deadlock. He decides to deadlock “charming,” turning it to “cold.” In a few moments, Pendleton will come back to their senses, albeit with a new, frosty demeanor.

Hadwig, an Operative, was cautiously hiding on the window ledge outside, watching all this happen (Olivia had declared this as hitting her key, marking “cautious” a third time, and marking 1 xp on her playbook track).

As Pendleton crumples to the ground and Veretta Keel flicks out a switchblade, Hadwig decides to intervene. Olivia declares that she’s going to smash through the window and tackle Veretta to the ground, with a goal of putting her silenced shooter to Veretta’s head. She’s thinking Prowl

for the action. The GM suggests that this will be desperate position, with limited effect (meaning, in this case, that the action will cover smashing the window and tackling Veretta, but a second action will be required to get the gun to her head). Hadwig is already at 7 Stress, so things are tense.

Olivia declares that she’s going to turn Hadwig’s key “cautious.” This is a moment of crisis, and Hadwig has been pushed to uncharacteristic recklessness. The action is an automatic critical success, which means increased effect—Hadwig smashes through the window, tackles Veretta to the ground, and puts her shooter to Veretta’s head. No roll needed. Hadwig clears all her Stress, and Olivia crosses out “cautious” and replaces it with “reckless.”

Stacks and Bank

Stacks

A stack is an abstract amount of money; as the name implies, a stack of cash. A week’s wages, a decent wad of bills.

We don’t track money below this amount. All PCs are assumed to have a pocketful of coins at any given time, maybe a few paper notes in their wallet. The crew as a whole has its own ledger of incomings and outgoings that we also do not track to the last coin.

If a character wants to spend money to achieve a small goal (bribe a bouncer), use the PC’s lifestyle quality for a fortune roll.

Monetary Values

  • 1 stack: A fat wad of bills. A week’s wages.

  • 2 stacks: A fine firearm. A fashionable suit.

  • 4 stacks: A month’s wages. A briefcase full of cash.

  • 6 stacks: A luxury watch or exquisite diamond. A stolen autopod.

  • 8 stacks: A small safe full of cash and valuables. A very rare luxury commodity (a first-edition book, an original vinyl pressing, a sought-after vintage from a rarefied vineyard).

  • 10 stacks: A significant asset—a luxury autopod, the deed to a small property, a painting by a world-famous artist, a yacht.

More than 4 stacks is an impractical amount to keep lying around. You must spend the excess or put it in the bank (see the next page).

A crew can also store 4 stacks in their base, by default. If they upgrade to a vault, they can expand their stores to 8 and then 16 stacks. Any stacks beyond their limit must be spent as soon as possible (typically before the next score) or distributed among the crew members.

A stack of cash takes up one item slot for your load when carried.

Spending Stacks

  • Spend 1 stack to get an additional downtime activity during personal business .

  • Spend 1 stack to increase the result level of a downtime activity roll.

  • Spend stacks to avoid certain crew entanglements.

  • Put stacks into your character’s bank to improve their lifestyle and circumstances when they retire.

  • Spend stacks when you advance your crew’s Tier .

Bank

When you mark your character’s final deadlock and they retire, the amount of cash they’ve managed to bank determines their fate. Your bank tracker is on your character sheet.

Like stacks, bank is an abstraction—it represents some combination of money stashed in physical hiding places, stocks, legitimate financial institutions, and underworld banks.

Retirement

  • Bank 0 – 10: Down and out. A derelict flophouse or crashing on couches.

  • Bank 11 – 20: Getting by. A modest home or apartment. You might run a bar or small business.

  • Bank 21 – 39: Comfortable. A stylish home or trendy apartment, with luxuries like a nice car or fashionable clothes. You might operate a medium business.

  • Bank 40: Fine living. A mansion, with the ability to afford luxuries like servants or a pleasure yacht. You no longer need to work if you don’t want to.

In addition, each full row of bank (10 stacks) indicates the Quality level of the character’s lifestyle , from zero (street life) to four (luxury). Primarily this flows into effect , especially in social situations. If you can take someone to a nice restaurant, if you’re wearing impressive clothes, all of this might factor into increased effect for social actions.

You can also roll lifestyle as a fortune roll —for example, when bribing a guard, you might make a lifestyle fortune roll first to see how big the bribe is. The outcome of that roll informs the subsequent action roll (whether Sway, or anything else), like a setup action.

Finally, when you roll to Acquire Asset during downtime, roll the crew’s Tier with bonus dice equal to your personal lifestyle quality (full rows of bank). The more you have in the bank, the easier it is to acquire assets.

Withdrawing Stacks from the Bank

If you want to pull stacks out of the bank, you may do so, at a cost. For every 2 stacks removed from the bank, you get 1 stack in cash.

Advancement

PC Advancement

During the game session , mark xp:

  • When you make a desperate action roll. When you roll in a group action that’s desperate, you also mark xp.

  • When you make an action roll with a zero-dot rating. This means any action you have no rating in. It’s OK if you then gain some bonus dice from pushing or assistance—you still mark xp. When you roll a zero-dot action as part of a group action, you also mark xp. An action that’s both desperate and zero-dot earns 2 xp.

  • Mark 1 xp when you hit any key (showcasing it in play), once per session . Tell the rest of the table when you do.

In all these cases, you can mark the xp on any track (an attribute like Prowess, or on your playbook xp track).

At the end of the session , review the xp triggers on your character sheet. For each one, mark 1 xp if it happened at all, or mark 2 xp if it happened a lot during the session. The xp triggers are:

  • Your playbook-specific xp trigger. For example, the Veteran’s is “Address a challenge with violence or discipline.” To “address a challenge,” your character should attempt to overcome a tough obstacle or threat. It doesn’t matter if the action is successful or not, you get xp either way.

  • You struggled with issues stemming from a deadlock. Mark xp for this if one of your deadlocks caused you trouble.

  • If you forgot to hit a key during the session, feel free to retroactively explain how you showcased one now, and mark 1 xp.

Mark end-of-session xp on any xp tracks you want (any attribute or your playbook xp track).

When you fill an xp track, clear all the marks and take an advance. When you take an advance from your playbook track, you may choose an additional special ability. When you take an advance from an attribute, you may add an additional action dot to one of the actions under that attribute.

Crew Advancement

Crew advancement works the same as usual. See Blades in the Dark , page 49.

Looking for Rep, hold, and increasing Tier? See Blades in the Dark , page 44.

Gameplay Cycle

Blades ’68 has its own gameplay cycle, different to core Blades in the Dark . Play moves through four typical phases, then cycles back around to the start.

1. Personal Business (page 164). Personal business is free play—the characters just living their lives. They talk to each other, they go places, they do things, they make rolls as needed. This is sprinkled with downtime activities, which give the PCs the opportunity to indulge their vice to remove Stress, recover harm, work on a long-term project, or achieve other specific goals. When all downtime activities are complete and the PCs have done as much as they want to with free play, the crew gathers to pick their next score.

2. Planning Meeting (page 180). The table collaborates to decide on the next score. Everyone pitches an opportunity, and then the players pick one to do next (and maybe also pick a secondary score for a cohort to tackle). Once a score is chosen, the GM will add some specific details to the broad opportunity.

3. The Score (page 188). The PCs might choose to gather some information, then when they’re ready, they choose an approach and make an engagement roll. The GM cuts to the score in progress. The PCs engage the target—they make rolls, overcome obstacles, call for flashbacks, and complete the operation (successfully or not).

4. Aftermath (page 190). During this short phase, we tally the fallout from the score, including payout, tithes, Rep, Heat, faction status changes, and harm complications. Aftermath ends with an unwind scene, as the crew debriefs and blows off steam together. The unwind scene is a capstone to the whole cycle, and transitions us back to the start (personal business).

Once per gameplay cycle, the GM will run the Trouble Engine between sessions. This involves checking in with faction clocks and rolling for new trouble (just like entanglements in core Blades ). Typically you might do this between gameplay cycles (after the aftermath and before personal business), but it’s flexible—do it whenever suits you best.

It’s personal preference how long you spend on each phase. Most tables give one session for the personal business phase and one session per score. This is extremely flexible—big scores can easily take multiple sessions. It’s helpful to finish personal business with the planning meeting, so the GM has time to think about the next score between sessions. Aftermath can be wedged in wherever it fits, either at the end of the score or before personal business.

Gameplay Cycle

Personal Business

Personal business is a mix of free play and downtime activities—weave them together. During the personal business phase, you can still go places, do things, make action rolls, gather information, talk with other characters, etc. Only activities that are on the downtime activities list are limited.

Personal business is the same as downtime in Blades in the Dark , and you can easily swap in any Deep Cuts modules that you like (such as diceless downtime).

Backing Factions: Return the Favor

If any PC has a backing faction, make a Return the Favor roll at the start of personal business. Use one die for each instance of help received since the last time you made this roll.

Return the Favor Results

See page 236 for more on backing factions, minor favors, and personal missions.

Downtime Activities

You get two free downtime activities , and can pay 1 stack or 1 Rep for each additional downtime activity:

  • Indulge Vice

  • Long-Term Project

  • Recover

  • Reduce Heat

  • Acquire Asset

  • Training Montage
